children.text: Stay in the loop
Get notified when new SEO insights are published. No spam, unsubscribe anytime.
Le Hoang Tin is an SEO Specialist with nearly 3 years of experience leading SEO projects at GTV SEO — one of Ho Chi Minh City's leading digital marketing agencies.

He specializes in data-driven SEO strategy, Conversion Rate Optimization (CRO), and optimizing content for AI-driven search channels like ChatGPT, Claude, and Google SGE. He has a particular interest in how AI is reshaping the way people search and discover information online.
Education
International Business, University of Economics Ho Chi Minh City (UEH) — GPA: 3.27/4.0
Skills
ChatGPT & Claude APIs — SEO content analysis & AI search optimization
Google Search Console & Screaming Frog — Technical SEO audits
SEMrush & Ahrefs — Keyword research & competitor analysis
Google Analytics 4 — Conversion tracking & user behavior analysis
SEO Project Management — KPI delivery, timeline management, client reporting
Recognition
3rd Prize — AI Application for SEO (GTV SEO internal competition)
Author of training documents and e-books for GTV SEO courses
Planned 10+ projects helping GTV SEO win new client bids (local and global markets)

data.title: How AI Search Changed Keyword Research Forever
data.excerpt: ChatGPT, Claude, and Google SGE don't rank pages — they synthesize answers. Here's how I've adapted my keyword research process for the era of AI search.

children.text: Two years ago, keyword research meant finding the right search volume and ranking difficulty balance. Today, when I research keywords for a client, I'm asking a different question: will this content get cited when someone asks ChatGPT about this topic?

The shift is real. According to data we've tracked at GTV SEO, a growing percentage of our clients' target audiences now get their first answer from an AI assistant — not from a Google results page. For informational queries especially, AI chat is the new first touch.
What changed and what didn't

What changed: the end destination. Users no longer reliably click through to websites for informational queries. AI models synthesize the answer inline, citing two or three sources. Being source #1 in the AI's response is the new Page 1 ranking.

What didn't change: the importance of depth, authority, and specificity. AI models cite pages that answer questions clearly and completely. Thin content fails in AI search for the same reason it failed in Google — it doesn't actually help the person asking.
My updated keyword research process

For every target keyword, I now run it through three tools: Google, ChatGPT, and Claude. The Google results tell me who's ranking. The AI responses tell me what answer is already considered authoritative enough to cite. The gap between the two is where the opportunity lives.

If a competing page ranks #1 on Google but doesn't appear in AI citations, that's a signal the content is technically optimized but lacks depth. If a source is consistently cited by AI but ranks #5 on Google, it's probably genuinely good content that deserves stronger distribution. Both are actionable insights.

data.title: 3 Years at GTV SEO: What I Learned
data.excerpt: From my first client audit to leading 10+ projects simultaneously — the honest lessons from building a career at one of Vietnam's leading SEO agencies.

$media.alt: Team working around a table with laptops

children.text: I joined GTV SEO Agency in April 2023 as a junior SEO executive. I had textbook knowledge, reasonable English, and a lot to prove. By the time I was leading national-scale campaigns for brands like Vinamilk, I understood why agencies either make you or break you — there's no slow lane.
Lesson 1: Clients don't buy SEO, they buy certainty

The first time I presented a strategy to a client, I focused entirely on the technical work — crawl budget optimization, internal linking, schema markup. They stopped me midway. They didn't care about schema. They cared about whether their phone would ring more in 90 days.

Every proposal I write now opens with the business problem, not the solution. The technical roadmap is page two. This shift — from SEO deliverables to business outcomes — changed how we win pitches. It's also the reason I helped GTV close 10+ new client bids.
Lesson 2: Speed beats perfection, always

Agency life teaches you to ship. In my first month I spent three days perfecting a content brief that should have taken half a day. My manager reviewed it in 20 minutes and sent it to the client. The client was happy. The extra two days of refinement changed nothing.
The best SEO results I've achieved weren't from perfect strategies. They were from good strategies executed fast, with weekly adjustments based on what the data was actually showing.
Lesson 3: GTV's own SEO was the hardest brief

The project I'm most proud of isn't a client campaign. It's GTV's own brand SEO. Getting our agency to rank #1 on ChatGPT, Claude, and Grok for 'SEO agency HCMC' — and maintaining Top 1–3 on Google for our main keywords — required a level of consistency and experimentation that no paid engagement would have allowed.

data.title: How I Hit Top 3 Rankings in 2 Weeks
data.excerpt: A case study on taking repaircentrevic.com.au from obscurity to Top 3–5 in Australia for iPhone and Samsung repair queries — in under two weeks.

$media.alt: Phone repair tools on a clean white desk

children.text: The client came to us in February 2025 with a website that had been live for 18 months but was invisible in search. Their target: rank for iPhone repair and Samsung repair keywords in Melbourne, Australia. Their timeline: as fast as possible.
The diagnosis

A Screaming Frog crawl immediately showed the problem: 47 pages, only 3 had unique meta descriptions, zero structured data, and the homepage title tag was 'Home — Welcome'. The site had authority — several decent backlinks from Australian tech directories — but Google had no idea what the site was actually about.
The fix

Week one was entirely on-page: rewrote every title tag and meta description with the target city and device model in the right positions, added LocalBusiness and Repair schema JSON-LD, and consolidated duplicate service pages into single authoritative URLs.

Week two: Google Business Profile optimization with photos, service categories, and a Q&A section answering the exact questions users search before booking a repair. We requested re-indexing via Search Console immediately after each batch of changes.
The result

By day 11, the site was showing in position 3–5 for 'iPhone repair Melbourne' and 'Samsung screen repair Melbourne'. We hit 85% of the agreed KPIs within the first month. The lesson: technical debt creates ranking debt. Paying it down quickly is usually enough to move fast.

data.title: AI Assistants Are the New Search Engines
data.excerpt: When your target customer asks ChatGPT instead of Googling, do they hear your brand's name? That's the question every SEO should be answering right now.

children.text: I tested something recently: I asked ChatGPT 'What are the best SEO agencies in Ho Chi Minh City?' and then asked the same question on Google. ChatGPT named GTV SEO first. Google showed a map pack. Different answers, different user experiences — but the same underlying question.

The reason ChatGPT named GTV first is because we had built the right content signals: articles, mentions in industry publications, structured data that made our services unambiguous to machines. Everything we did for traditional SEO turned out to also work for AI citation — it just works through a different mechanism.
How AI models decide what to cite

Large language models are trained on a snapshot of the web, plus they can retrieve content in real time (for models with browsing). Citations tend to go to pages that are: clear about what they are (schema helps), consistent across sources (your brand name appearing the same way everywhere matters), and genuinely useful to someone asking that specific question.
What to do about it

I recommend clients audit their brand presence in AI tools quarterly: ask ChatGPT and Claude about their target keywords and see if they appear. If they don't, the gap is usually either: no content that directly answers the question at depth, or inconsistent brand signals across the web. Both are fixable with traditional SEO methods.

data.title: My Technical SEO Toolkit: What I Use Every Week
data.excerpt: Screaming Frog, GSC, Ahrefs, and a few less obvious tools. Here's the exact stack I use for technical audits and how each one earns its place.

$media.alt: Developer tools and analytics on a screen

children.text: Every SEO has their tool stack. Mine has stabilized over the past year into a core set of four tools that I use on every project, plus a few specialized ones that I pull out when the situation calls for it. Here's what I use and why.
Screaming Frog — the foundation

Every new client engagement starts with a full crawl. Screaming Frog gives me a complete picture of the site's technical health in minutes: missing titles and descriptions, duplicate content, broken links, redirect chains, orphaned pages. I use the custom extraction feature to pull structured data from pages and verify schema implementation.
Google Search Console — the ground truth

GSC is the only tool that shows you what Google actually thinks about your site. I use it primarily for: identifying pages with impressions but low CTR (title/description problem), finding keywords where a page ranks 5–15 (quick win candidates), and monitoring Core Web Vitals trends after site changes.
Ahrefs — for the competitive picture

Ahrefs is my primary tool for backlink analysis and keyword research. The Site Explorer shows me a competitor's full organic footprint — what they rank for, what content drives their traffic, where their links come from. That competitive intelligence shapes about 40% of my content strategy recommendations.
ChatGPT & Claude — the new research layer

I use AI assistants to understand how topics are framed in conversational search, to identify semantic gaps in existing content, and to quickly draft content briefs. They've replaced about half of my manual research time. More importantly, testing a client's target keywords in ChatGPT and Claude has become a standard part of every competitive audit.
